title: Almost Palindrome
prompt:
Given a string s, return true if it can become a palindrome by removing at most one character.

Palindrome rules for this problem:
- Consider only alphanumeric characters (a–z, A–Z, 0–9).
- Ignore case (treat 'A' and 'a' as equal).

Notes:
- Empty strings and single characters are palindromes.
- You may remove 0 or 1 alphanumeric character; non-alphanumeric characters are ignored either way.

Return a boolean.
constraintQuestions: If the string is already a palindrome, should we return true? (Yes.)
Should we aim for O(n) time and O(1) extra space?

walkthrough.title: Preprocessing (real-world palindrome rules)
walkthrough.body:
Because interviews often treat palindromes as 'alphanumeric only' and case-insensitive:
1) Remove non-alphanumeric characters.
2) Convert to lowercase.
Now the string is safe to compare with two pointers.

walkthrough.title: Core idea (palindrome as a subproblem)
walkthrough.body:
Scan with two pointers from the outside in.
If characters match, move inward.
If a mismatch appears at (L, R), you have one allowed removal:
- Either skip L (check substring L+1..R)
- Or skip R (check substring L..R-1)
If either substring is a palindrome, the whole string is an almost-palindrome.
Time: O(n)
Space: O(1)

tests.name: Already palindrome (classic)
tests.s: A man, a plan, a canal: Panama

tests.name: Cannot be fixed with one removal
